coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and luminescence properties of carbon quantum dots with core@shell structures
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Juan-Rong
Li, Jing
Tang, Xiao-Min
Chu, Wen-Xin
An, Bao-Li
Zhang, Ji-Ming
Wang, Xiao-Hong
Bai, Yue-Ling
Xu, Jiaqiang
coredata.dc:description: The luminescence stability of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in air impedes their extensive applications. Ample –NH2 groups on the surface of a green carbon quantum dots (GCQDs) had high organic reactivity, the surface of GCQDs was modified with allyl functional groups through a simple surface substitution reaction (named as GCQD-A). The surface of the GCQD-A was continuously formed a thin organic shell by free radical copolymerization reaction with methyl methacrylate and styrene respectively, and they were named as GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS respectively. The allyl functional groups on the surface of GCQD-A, the shell composition of GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS were characterized by their 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) data and infrared adsorption spectra. The luminescence stabilities for GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS in air were greatly improved compared with that for the GCQDs. The work can provide a novel method to get stable luminescence of CQDs in air, and it will extend their application areas in solid state.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Surface modification
Core@shell structure
Luminescence
Luminescence stability

National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No.62271299). The authors also acknowledge the Instrumental Analysis and Research Center of Shanghai University for providing measurement services.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for the synthesis of GCQD-A, GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS
The TEM images and particle size distribution diagrams for GCQD-A (a, b), GCQD@PMMA (c, d) and GCQD@PS (e, f). The inset inserted in Fig. 2a showed the HRTEM images for GCQD-A.
1H NMR spectra for (a) GCQD-A and (b) GCQD-A-P (400 MHz, CDCl3).
FT-IR spectra of GCQDs, GCQD-A, GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS.
UV–vis absorption spectra of GCQDs, GCQD-A, GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS in isopropanol dilute solutions.

The excitation (a) and emission (b) spectra for 1.0 mg/ml GCQDs, 1.0 mg/ml GCQD-A, 1.0 mg/ml GCQD@PMMA and 1.0 mg/ml GCQD@PS in isopropanol solutions (both the excitation slit and the emission slit were 0.7 nm).
Fluorescence lifetime decay curves of (a) GCQDs, (b) GCQD-A, (c) GCQD@PMMA and (d) GCQD@PS in isopropanol dilute solutions.

(a) The fluorescence stabilities of GCQDs, GCQD-A, GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS in dilute isopropyl alcohol solutions. (b)The fluorescence photos for GCQDs, GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS in air under both a 365 nm UV light and natural white light.
The Stoke's shifts for GCQDs, GCQD-A, GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS in isopropanol dilute solutions.
The quantum yield data for GCQDs, GCQD-A, GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS in isopropanol dilute solutions.
Fluorescence lifetime data for GCQDs, GCQD-A, GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS in isopropanol dilute solutions.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and luminescence properties of carbon quantum dots with core@shell structures

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Shanghai University, Shanghai, 200444, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Periodicals Agency, Shanghai University, Shanghai, 200444, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The luminescence stability of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in air impedes their extensive applications. Ample –NH2 groups on the surface of a green carbon quantum dots (GCQDs) had high organic reactivity, the surface of GCQDs was modified with allyl functional groups through a simple surface substitution reaction (named as GCQD-A). The surface of the GCQD-A was continuously formed a thin organic shell by free radical copolymerization reaction with methyl methacrylate and styrene respectively, and they were named as GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS respectively. The allyl functional groups on the surface of GCQD-A, the shell composition of GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS were characterized by their 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) data and infrared adsorption spectra. The luminescence stabilities for GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS in air were greatly improved compared with that for the GCQDs. The work can provide a novel method to get stable luminescence of CQDs in air, and it will extend their application areas in solid state.

The luminescence stabilities in air for GCQDs were greatly enhanced by coating a very thin organic polymer shells on the surfaces of GCQDs.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are an emerging class of zero-dimensional nanomaterials that have attracted much attention due to their strong luminescent properties, biocompatibility, uniform particle size, low-cost synthesis, and highly tunable photoluminescence [1]. CQDs are composed of carbon nuclei with some functional groups on the surfaces [2]. The synthesis methods for CQDs have been reviewed [3,4]. CQDs with uniform particle size could be synthesized by hydrothermal and solvothermal method, presently, visible and near-infrared CQDs have been synthesized by hydrothermal methods [5–7].

At present, there is no agreement on the luminescence mechanism of CQDs, and three luminescence mechanisms for CQDs are extensively accepted [3]. One is that the emission comes from the band gap states of CQDs [8], and the size-dependent intrinsic emission from the graphene core with sp2 clusters was attributed to the quantum confinement effects of the carbon nucleus [9]. The second is that the emission comes from the surface-related defect states, which is ascribed to the radiative recombination of the surface-confined electrons and the holes of the CQDs. The surface of the carbon nuclei is modified with some functional groups, such as amino, epoxy, aldehyde, hydroxy and carboxyl groups, and the surface states dominate the luminescence of the CQDs in most cases [10]. In these cases, the maximum emission wavelengths were independent on the synthesis time and the synthesis temperature which generally affect the size of CQDs [11]. The third is that the emission comes from the molecular state emissions of carbon quantum dots by imbedding some organic molecular fluorophores on the surfaces of the CQDs [12,13].

Generally, the emission color of CQDs can be regulated by selecting carbon source of chemicals, changing the polarity of the solvents [14,15]. In our presently published work, the surface band gap states of a green CQDs were successfully tuned gradually by modifying the surface defect state with functional groups, and the emission colors of the green CQDs were tuned from green to yellow-green, then to orange, and finally to red. The red-shift multi-colors for the CQDs were attributed to the emissions from their surface defect band states [16].

CQDs have been widely applied to fluorescence detection [17], bio-imaging [18], anti-counterfeiting [19], diagnosis [20]. CQDs have attracted much attention in white-emitting diodes due to their broad emission, tunable colors and high thermal stability together with low cost [21]. We have synthesized a bright yellow green CQDs with big Stokes shift of 150 nm, and the yellow green CQDs could be used as a sensitive fluorescence probe of folic acid [22].

Surface modification is essential for both improving the luminescence properties of CQDs and extending their applications. Functional surface groups such as –OH, –NH2, –COOH provide active sites for further modifications [23]. Multi-color CQDs@SiO2 were prepared by “reversed-phase microemulsion” method, and the luminescence properties of the CQDs were preserved in the solid state of the CQDs by encapsulated with silica shell [24]. Blue CQDs were imbedded into polystyrene and polymethyl methacrylate with N,N-dimethylformamide as a solvent [25].

We found that the fluorescence intensity of the synthesized GCQDs decreased gradually in the air, the reason was inferred that the surface defect states of GCQDs were easily affected by oxygen and water molecules. Because the groups on the surface of GCQDs were easy to be oxidized, the unstable surface groups will produce fluorescence decline [26]. It is essential to passivate the surface defect states of CQDs for their extensive application in solid state. The core@shell structure of carbon quantum dots can protect their surface defect states with good luminescence stability, moreover, the shell can also introduce functional groups as for wider applications. However, due to the small diameter of CQD, it is difficult to coat a polymer shell on the core carbon nucleus accurately. The thickness of the polymer shell is vital to the luminescence properties of CQDs. The carbon core will not be protected well in air with too thin polymer shell. Moreover, the luminescence will decline visibly due to the refraction of the excitation light by too thick polymer shell. Presently, it is still a challenge to synthesize core@shell structure of CQDs with a proper thin polymer shell.

In this work, based on the high organic reactivity of –NH2 groups on the surface of a green carbon quantum dots (GCQDs), the surface of GCQDs was modified with allyl functional groups through an organic reaction. Then, the modified GCQDs were co-polymerized with methyl methacrylate (MMA) and styrene (S) respectively, and core@shell structured carbon quantum dots, GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS, were synthesized respectively. The luminescence stabilities for GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS in air were greatly improved compared with that for the GCQDs.

Tin tetrachloride pentahydrate and m-phenylenediamine were of 99.5 % purity. Isopropanol was HPLC grade, methyl methacrylate (MMA) was 95 % purity. ethanol, allyl bromide, styrene, 2,2-azobis(2-methylpropionitrile) (AIBN), potassium carbonate, n-tetrabutylammonium bromide, sodium hydroxide, polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) and 2,6-di-tert-butyl-p-cresol were of 99.0 % purity. All chemicals above were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Corporation of China. All the reagents were used without any purification.
GCQDs was synthesized and purified referring to our published work [11]. The final GCQDs in isopropanol solution (30 ml, 22 mg/ml) was stored in refrigerator for further use.

25 ml GCQDs solution was dispersed in a mixture of 30.0 ml isopropanol and 1.0 ml deionized water. The mixture was sonicated for 10 min, then it was transferred to a three-neck flask. 0.828 g (6.0 mmol) K2CO3, 5.0 mg n-tetrabutylammonium bromide were added in the flask under stirring. After the flask was purged with nitrogen gas for 30 min, 0.488 g (4.0 mmol) allyl bromide was injected dropwise by syringe. The mixture was refluxed at 80 °C in oil bath for about 18 h. Since the polarity for GCQD-A was smaller than that for GCQDs, the reaction product was monitored by thin layer chromatography (TLC) until the substitution reaction of –NH2 groups on the surface of GCQDs was completed. The solvents were removed by a rotary evaporator, and the syrupy product was dispersed in 25 ml isopropanol. K2CO3 and the insoluble particles were removed by filtration. The filtrate was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (molecular weight of 3500 Da) for 24 h, replacing the solvent every 12 h. After the soluble small molecules were removed by dialysis, 25 ml GCQD-A in isopropanol solution was obtained. The synthesis scheme for GCQD-A is shown in Fig. 1 .

The solvent for 7.5 ml 22 mg/ml GCQD-A solution was removed by a rotary evaporator, and the residue was dispersed in a mixture of 10 ml ethanol and 1.0 ml deionized water. 5.0 mg AIBN, 5.0 mg PVP and 5.0 mg n-tetrabutylammonium bromide were added into the solution, and the temperature of the solution was increased slowly to 64 °C under nitrogen gas atmosphere. Then, 0.300 g (3.0 mmol) methyl methacrylate (MMA) was slowly injected into the solution, and it was stirred at 64 °C for 24 h. The polymerization reaction was stopped by adding 5.0 mg ethanol solution of 2,6-di-tert-butyl-p-cresol, and the solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (molecular weight of 3500 Da) for 24 h. After the small molecules were removed by dialysis, ethanol solution of GCQD@PMMA was obtained.

The synthesis and purification method for GCQD@PS were similar to those for GCQD@PMMA, just replacing the MMA with 3.0 mmol styrene as polymeric monomer. Ethanol solution of GCQD@PS was obtained. The synthesis scheme for GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS are shown in Fig. 1.

The morphologies and sizes were obtained on a TEM-2100F transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) with accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Nuclear magnetic resonance hydrogen spectroscopy (1H NMR) spectra were recorded on JEOL-400MHz. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were performed on a Thermo Nicolet AVATRA 370 infrared spectrophotometer. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra were obtained on a TU-1950 ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer. The emission and excitation spectra were performed on an Edinburgh FLS 1000 transient steady-state fluorescence spectrometer. The time-resolved fluorescence decay curves were obtained on FLS 1000 with EPL-UV 375 nm laser or EPL-Visible 450 nm laser as the excitation source. All the life time data were obtained by auto calculation of FSL 1000. The quantum yield data were determined on FLS 1000 with a Red PMT sphere.

The morphologies and size distributions for GCQDs were reported in our published work. The XRD pattern of GCQDs showed a broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 25.9o, which is attributed to the (002) diffraction patterns of the graphitic carbon [11]. The morphology of GCQD-A is shown in Fig. 2 (a) . The shape was close to spherical with uniform particle size distribution and no aggregation phenomenon. GCQD-A had good crystallinity with obvious lattice stripes, and the lattice spacing was 0.21 nm, which was ascribed to the (100) in-plane lattice of graphitic carbon [27]. The particles distribution for GCQD-A is given in Fig. 2 (b), and the average particle size is 2.66 nm.

The TEM morphologies and size distributions for GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS are given in Fig. 2(c and d) and (e, f) respectively. All the nano particles for GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS showed good dispersibility without obvious aggregation, and the particles were close to quasi-spherical. The insets inserted in Fig. 2c and e showed the TEM images for GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS respectively. Compared with that for GCQD-A, the average size for GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS was increased to 3.5 nm, and 3.7 nm respectively, and they all could dispersed uniformly in isopropanol solution, tetrahydrofuran, ethanol, water with no agglomeration. The particles had a polymer shell on the surfaces of their carbon nucleus with average size of 2.66 nm, and the average polymer shell thickness for GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS was 0.84 nm and 1.04 nm respectively. Since the density of the carbon nucleus is larger than that of the polymer shell, the black spherical shape is ascribed to the carbon core, and the white circle is attributed to the organic polymer shell.

In order to impede self-polymerization of the monomers, the solvent of isopropanol was evaporated. The surfaces of GCQD-A and the monomers were hydrophobic, and they all dispersed in a mixture solution of 10 ml ethanol and 1.0 ml deionized water. A complicated microemulsion solution was formed, and 5.0 mg PVP and 5.0 mg n-tetrabutylammonium bromide were designed as morphology agent and phase-transfer catalyst respectively. The water molecules could efficiently impede the self-polymerization of the monomers, both methyl methacrylate and styrene were successfully copolymerized respectively on the surface of GCQD-A.

The abundant –NH2 and –CH3 groups on the surface of GCQDs was confirmed by infrared absorption spectroscopy (FT-IR), 1H NMR spectrum and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) in our recently published work [11,16]. In this paper, the surface groups of GCQD-A were further characterized by 1H NMR spectroscopy (Fig. 3 ).

The H atoms in the allyl structure are marked with numbers in Fig. 3. Two distinct proton peaks between 9–12 ppm were attributed to the -NH- groups on the surface (Fig. 3a). It was inferred that one H atom of –NH2 groups in two different environments on the surface of GCQDs was substituted by allyl groups. In contrast, as the two H atoms of –NH2 groups were substituted completely by allyl groups, the -NH- proton peaks between 9–12 ppm would not be observed. Thus, the GCQD-A was purified by column chromatography with petroleum (60–90 °C) and ether as an eluent, a purified component with less polar was obtained (named as GCQD-A-P). The proton peaks between 9–12 ppm were not observed in the 1H NMR spectrum for GCQD-A-P (Fig. 3b). Fig. 3b showed that the two H atoms for some of the –NH2 groups on the surface of GCQDs were substituted completed by allyl groups. As shown in Fig. 3b, the typical duplex peaks at 3.885 ppm (4H) for H1 further proved that the two H atoms on the –NH2 group on the surface of GCQDs were completely substituted by allyl groups, otherwise the 1H NMR of H1 should be a triple peak due to the spin coupling effect of H2 and the H atoms of the –NH- groups. Since the two hydrogen atoms of cis- and trans-olefins (cis-H3a, trans-H3b) were not free to rotate, they each split into two peaks by the spin coupling of H2, H3a (5.185, 1H, 3 J = 12 Hz) and H3b (5.140, 1H, 3 J = 8 Hz), and the neighboring H3a was subject to the spin coupling of H2 more than the opposite H3b. H2 was subject to both H1 and H3 spin coupling, and theoretically H2 split into a quintuple peak. Since the two hydrogen atoms of cis-H3a and trans-H3b were not free to rotate, H2 actually split into a double quadruple peak (1H) at 5.865 ppm. The δ values of the H1, H2, H3a, and H3b for GCQD-A-P and GCQD-A were reduced by 0.035–0.16 ppm compared to those peaks of the standard 1H NMR spectrum for AB molecule in the comprehensive spectral database system of organic compounds, and the reduced δ values of the proton peaks for GCQD-A-P and GCQD-A further confirmed that allyl groups were successfully modified to the surface of GCQDs.

The 1H NMR spectra for GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS are given in the supporting information (Fig. S1). In the 1H NMR spectrum for GCQD@PMMA (Fig. S1a), the single peak at 3.59 ppm (3H) is attributed to methyl Ha on the ester group of PMMA, the single peak at 0.98 ppm (3H) is ascribed to methyl group of Hb of PMMA, and the two peaks at 1.01 ppm and 1.25 ppm (2H) are attributed to the methylene Hc of PMMA. In addition, the peaks in the range of 1.50–2.00 ppm are attributed to the methylene CH2- and methyne –CH– on the polymerization chain. The characteristic peaks for the olefin hydrogen in the range of 5.00–6.00 ppm were not observed, which confirmed that MMA was successfully polymerized on the surfaces of GCQDs.

Similarly, the characteristic peaks for the olefin hydrogen in the range of 5.00–6.00 ppm were not observed in the 1H NMR spectrum for GCQD@PS, which confirmed that styrene was successfully polymerized on the surfaces of GCQDs (Fig. S1b). However, the copolymerization structures of GCQD@PS were complicated. At least two main copolymerization structures on the surfaces of GCQD@PS were inferred from its 1H NMR spectrum, and the two structures were named GCQD@PS-1 and GCQD@PS-2 as showed in Fig. S1b. The peaks, 7.565 (1H, d, J = 4.0 Hz), 7.38 (2H, t, J = 4.0 Hz) and 7.13 (2H, d, d, 3 J = 8.0 Hz, 4 J = 4.0 Hz) were ascribed to GCQD@PS-1 structure. The peaks, 7.305 (2H, d, J = 4.0 Hz), 7.23 (1H, t, J = 4.0 Hz), 7.20 (1H, t, J = 4.0 Hz), and 7.18 (1H, d, d, 3 J = 8.0 Hz, 4 J = 4.0 Hz) were attributed to GCQD@PS-2 structure. Based on the relative integrated hydrogen peak area, the content for GCQD@PS-1 was about 4 times of that for GCQD@PS-2, the leading structure of GCQD@PS-1 will be further confirmed by the following IR spectrum of GCQD@PS.

As shown in Fig. 4 , both GCQDs and GCQD-A have three strong absorption peaks at 2974 cm−1, 2888 cm−1 and 1383 cm−1, corresponding to the asymmetric stretching vibration (νas, C–H), the symmetric stretching vibration (νs, C–H) and the symmetric bending vibration (δ s, C–H) of the C–H bond of –CH3 groups. As the-NH2 group for GCQDs was substituted by allyl group, the characteristic double narrow absorption peaks for the-NH2 group became single narrow absorption peak at 3655 cm−1 for -NH- group of GCQD-A. Besides, a distinct absorption peak at 1605 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibrational absorption peak of the CC bond of allyl group, and the absorption peak at 1077 cm−1 was attributed to the out-of-plane bending vibration absorption of C–H of olefin. Therefore, the FT-IR data of GCQD-A wee consistent with its 1H NMR data, which further confirmed that the allyl group was successfully modified to the surfaces of GCQDs.

As shown in Fig. 4, the absorption peak of the stretching vibration of the CC bond at 1605 cm−1 was not observed in the FT-IR spectrum for GCQD@PMMA. The two peaks at 1728 cm−1 and 1664 cm−1 were ascribed to the vibrational absorption peaks of CO of GCQD@PMMA, and the absorption peaks at 1187 cm−1 was attributed to the vibrational absorption of C–O bond of GCQD@PMMA. The two wider peaks at 2920 cm−1 and 2860 cm−1 were ascribed to the asymmetric and symmetric vibrational absorption peaks of C–H bonds of –CH2- and–CH3 groups. Therefore, the FT-IR data of GCQD@PMMA further confirmed that a PMMA shell was successfully coted onto the surfaces of GCQDs.

As for the spectrum of GCQD@PS (Fig. 4), two weak absorption peaks at 3071 cm−1 and 3014 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibration absorption peaks of the C–H of the benzene ring, and the two strong peaks at 1605 cm−1 and 1501 cm−1 were attributed to the characteristic skeletal stretching vibration absorption peaks of the benzene ring. The weak double frequency absorption peaks out of the plane bending vibration of benzene at 1947 cm−1, 1886 cm−1, 1818 cm−1, 1747 cm−1 together with the strong absorption peaks out of the plane bending vibration of benzene at 810 cm−1, 744 cm−1, 693 cm−1 confirmed that GCQD@PS-1 was the main structure of GCQD@PS, and this result was consistent with its 1H NMR result.

The UV–vis absorption spectra for GCQDs, GCQD-A, GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS are shown in Fig. 5 . The peaks for GCQDs at 259 nm and 295 nm were attributed to the π–π* transitions, while the peak at 462 nm was ascribed to the electron transitions of the surface defect band states [14]. Compared with those for GCQDs, the π–π* absorption peaks for GCQD-A, GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS shifted to 264 nm and 308 nm, and absorption peaks from the surface defect band states for GCQD-A, GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS shifted to 477 nm due to the larger conjugated structures formed by the substitution of allyl groups on the surface of the carbon dots. However, the allyl group could slightly tune the surface defect energy of GCQDs, the surface defect energies for GCQDs have been confirmed to be reduced gradually by modifying functional groups on the surface of GCQDs [16]. Generally, due to the surface defect states are affected easily by solvent molecules, its absorption band is wider and weaker than the absorption bands from π–π* and n–π* transitions. Compared with those for GCQD-A, the maximum adsorption wavelengths for GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS did not change invisibly, indicating that the defect energy level states for GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS were not affected by the organic polymer shell layers on the surfaces of GCQD-A nanoparticles. The peak centered at 412 nm for GCQDs was ascribed to the absorption from the excited band states of the carbon core, and emission band centered at 425 nm was observed under excitation at 365 nm [16]. Compared with that for GCQDs, the peaks from the excited carbon core states for GCQD-A, GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS red shifted to 414 nm, 419 nm and 421 nm respectively. However, the π–π* absorption bands at 308 nm for GCQDA, GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS were broader than that for GCQDs, which was attributed to the refection from the organic polymer shells.

The excitation and emission spectra for 1.0 mg/ml GCQDs, GCQD-A, GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS in isopropanol dilute solutions are shown in Fig. 6 . The maximum emission wavelengths for GCQDs, GCQD-A, GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS were 507 nm, 505 nm, 509 nm and 511 nm respectively, and the emission spectra for GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS were broader than that for GCQD-A. The maximum excitation wavelength for GCQDs was 462 nm, and the maximum excitation wavelengths for GCQD-A, GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS red shifted to 477 nm. The maximum emission intensity of GCQD-A was close to that of GCQDs, and the maximum emission intensities for both GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS were around 92 % versus that of GCQDs. The excitation peak at 407 nm for GCQD-A, GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS was ascribed to the absorptions from the excited band states of the carbon cores which had visible absorption peaks at around 412–421 nm (Fig. 5). The emissions from the carbon cores of GCQD-A, GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS were observed under excitation wavelength at 380 nm, 378 nm and 372 nm respectively, and the emission peaks for GCQD-A, GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS centered at 433 nm, 433 nm and 428 nm (Fig. S2). The emission intensities from the carbon cores have been showed to be less sensitive to the emissions from the surface defect states [16].

The Stoke's shifts for 1.0 mg/ml GCQDs, GCQD-A, GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS in isopropanol solutions were shown in Table 1 . Compared with that for GCQD-A, the Stoke's-2 shifts for GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS increased slightly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The quantum yields and the lifetimes
ce:section.ce:para: The absolute quantum yield values for GCQDs, GCQD-A, GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS in isopropanol dilute solutions were determined to be 55.2 %, 51.7 %, 35.6 % and 31.0 % respectively, and the results were given in Table 2 .

The luminescence lifetimes were determined by recording the lifetime decay curves at the maximum wavelengths at 507 nm, 505 nm, 509 nm and 511 nm for GCQDs, GCQD-A, GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS in isopropanol dilute solutions respectively with EPL 450 nm laser as the excitation light source, and their luminescence decay curves were given in Fig. 7 . All the lifetime data were obtained by auto-calculation with the fluorescence instrument of FSL 1000, and the data were given in Table 3 . All the decay curves were fitted perfectly in second exponential decay process, and the equation was given in equations (3)–(1).
(3-1)
I
I
0 + A 1e(−t
/τ1) + A 2e(−t
/τ2)
where I is the luminescence intensity, I
0 is the photo count of the baseline of the decay curves, t is the decay time, τ
1 and τ
2 are the first-order decay lifetime and the second-order decay lifetime, and A
1 and A
2 are the percentages occupied by τ
1 and τ
2 , respectively. Compared with the τ
1 and τ
2 for GCQDs, both the τ
1 and τ
2 values for GCQD-A, GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS decreased slightly (Table 3). Compared with that for GCQDs, the average lifetimes for GCQD-A, GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS decreased 6.6 %, 11.3 % and 9.4 % respectively. As the temperature increased, the non-radiative decay ratio increased, and the quantum yield decreased together with declined τ
1 and τ
2 values. The reflecting and scattering from the organic polymer shell of PMMA and PS might provoke the temperature increasing, and the increased non-radiative decay ratio led to decreased quantum yields.

Usually, carbon quantum dots have very small particle size and high surface defect, and the surface defect states are susceptible to oxygen and water molecules in air, as a result, their fluorescence intensity decreases with the time. Organic polymer shells were designed to protect the surface defect states of GCQDs in order to improve its fluorescence stability in air. The fluorescence stabilities for GCQDs, GCQD-A, GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS in isopropyl alcohol are given in Fig. 8 (a) . The F 0 values were the maximum emission intensities of the initial samples, and the F values were the maximum emission intensities of the samples after several days. The stability for GCQD-A was visibly better than that for GCQDs, the reason was attributed to the hydrophobic allyl groups which might protect the surface defect states of GCQD-A. The fluorescence intensity for GCQD@PMMA did not decline in 6 days, and the fluorescence intensity for GCQD@PS was not observed decay in 7 days.

GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PC shown good fluorescence stabilities in air due to the formations of core@shell structures (Fig. 8 (b)). The solutions of GCQDs, GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS were dripped onto a silica gel thin layer chromatography plate with capillary tubes, and the plate was placed in air naturally. Fig. 8 gives the fluorescence photos of GCQDs, GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS in air under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp. The fluorescence intensity for GCQDs gradually decreased in air, and its fluorescence nearly disappeared in 5 h. However, the fluorescence intensity for GCQD@PMMA slightly decayed after 8 days (197 h), and the fluorescence intensity for GCQD@PS was not observed to decay in air over 12 days (293 h).

The polymer shell thickness for GCQD@PS was larger 0.2 nm than that for GCQD@PMMA, and the polymer shell could perfectly protect the surface defect states of GCQD@PS with good stability in air and in solutions. The fluorescence stabilities in isopropyl alcohol solution were consistent with those in air.

GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS with core@shell structures were successfully synthesized, and their fluorescence stabilities in air were greatly improved by protecting the surface defect states of GCQDs with the very thin organic polymer shells. The maximum emission wavelengths for GCQD-A, GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS were 505 nm, 509 nm, and 511 nm respectively, and the emission wavelengths for GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS red shifted slightly. The absolute quantum yields for GCQD-A, GCQD@PMMA and GCQD@PS were 51.7 %, 35.6 % and 31.0 % respectively. It provides a new method to protect the excited surface defect band states from air to increase the fluorescence stability in air. The results are beneficial for extensive applications of carbon quantun dots.

Juan-Rong Li: Writing – original draft. Jing Li: Data curation. Xiao-Min Tang: Formal analysis. Wen-Xin Chu: Data curation. Bao-Li An: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Ji-Ming Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Xiao-Hong Wang: Investigation. Yue-Ling Bai: Formal analysis. Jiaqiang Xu: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests:

Jiaqiang Xu reports financial support was provided by National Natural Science Foundation of China. Bao-Li An has patent #ZL 2021 1 1488851.1 licensed to Shanghai University. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No.62271299). The authors also acknowledge the Instrumental Analysis and Research Center of Shanghai University for providing measurement services.
